content.text: Discovery workshops for generative AI features succeed or fail based on the quality of preparation before participants arrive and the discipline of capture after they leave. Workshop facilitation artefacts — templates, canvases, decision logs, and structured outputs — are the operational layer that converts an energy-filled session into a reliable product input.
What It Is

Workshop facilitation artefacts are the collection of documents, templates, and structured outputs that surround a discovery workshop for an AI feature. They fall into four categories: pre-workshop preparation artefacts that set up the session for success, live artefacts that the facilitator uses or generates during the session, post-session capture artefacts that preserve the session outcome, and backlog artefacts that translate the session output into implementable work items.

For generative AI and agentic system development, the artefact set must be adapted beyond what standard Agile workshops produce. GenAI features require additional artefacts that capture model behaviour constraints, prompt engineering hypotheses, safety acceptance criteria, and LLM-specific failure modes — none of which appear in a standard sprint planning template or story map.

The facilitation artefact set is not a one-size-fits-all toolkit. Different session types (Event Storming, Design Sprint, impact mapping, threat modelling) require different artefact configurations. The focus here is on the artefacts that cut across most GenAI feature discovery workshops, with emphasis on the adaptations specific to LLM-based systems.
Why It Matters

Unstructured workshops waste the most valuable resource in a product organisation: the simultaneous attention of multiple senior experts. A two-hour session with ten people costs twenty person-hours. Without a clear agenda, shared sticky-note vocabulary, a hotspot capture mechanism, and a defined post-session conversion process, that investment frequently yields only a photograph of a whiteboard that no one refers to again.

For AI feature discovery specifically, the risks of poor artefact discipline are compounded. AI capabilities are harder to specify than CRUD features, which means the quality of the session output has outsized impact on whether the implementation correctly addresses user intent. A missing safety constraint that was discussed verbally in the session but not captured in an artefact will not appear in acceptance criteria and will not be tested — and may ship as a live defect.
Benefits
Structured preparation templates
reduce the facilitator's cognitive load during the session, freeing them to manage group dynamics rather than remembering what to prompt participants to do next.
Standardised sticky-note legends
prevent the vocabulary drift that causes one participant's "event" to mean what another calls a "command," which corrupts the board's causal structure.
Hotspot tally sheets
ensure that every risk and knowledge gap surfaced in the session is assigned an owner and a resolution deadline before participants leave.
Backlog conversion templates
prevent the session output from sitting in a Miro board unread — they provide the mapping from board elements to epics, stories, acceptance criteria, and spike tasks.
AI-specific artefact additions
(prompt constraint canvases, model behaviour specification sheets) surface requirements that standard Agile artefacts are not designed to capture.
Trade-offs and Limitations
Over-templating can make a workshop feel bureaucratic and reduce the spontaneous exploration that generates the most valuable insights — artefacts should scaffold the session, not script it.

Artefact maintenance overhead: templates must be updated as the team learns what works for their specific domain; stale templates become actively harmful if they lead participants to skip steps that the current version assumed they would take.

Digital-first artefacts (Miro boards, shared Google Docs) require reliable connectivity and tool familiarity; in distributed sessions, technical friction with the artefact tooling can consume facilitation time.

Capture artefacts are only as good as the quality of post-session synthesis — if the product manager who converts the board to backlog items was not present in the session, important context will be lost.
How It Works

The artefact lifecycle has four stages that map to the workshop timeline: preparation (24–72 hours before), live session (during), immediate capture (within two hours after), and backlog conversion (within 24 hours after).
Pre-Workshop Preparation Artefacts

The agenda template defines the workshop structure with time-boxed phases, phase objectives, transition signals, and the facilitator actions required at each step. For a three-hour GenAI discovery workshop, a typical agenda includes: 10 minutes for context-setting, 30 minutes for chaotic event exploration, 45 minutes for timeline ordering and hotspot placement, 30 minutes for bounded context identification, 20 minutes for aggregate naming, and 25 minutes for backlog extraction. The agenda template also lists the materials the facilitator must prepare and the digital board setup steps.

The participant brief is a one-page document sent to attendees 48 hours before the session. It explains the workshop format, what participants are expected to contribute, and the pre-reading required. For AI feature workshops, the brief should include a short explanation of the sticky-note colour system and why events must be written in past tense. Without this briefing, a significant portion of facilitation time is spent explaining the format to participants who encounter it for the first time.

The scoping canvas defines the boundary of the session: which AI capability is in scope, which users and actors are in scope, what the success criteria for the feature look like, and what explicit exclusions apply. For GenAI features, the canvas includes a field for the target LLM or model family, the context window constraints, the tools the agent is allowed to invoke, and the human-in-the-loop checkpoints required by policy.
Live Session Artefacts

The sticky-note legend is a reference card displayed prominently on the board (or in the digital board's sidebar) that defines what each colour means. A standard GenAI workshop legend includes: orange for domain events (past tense), pink for commands (imperative), yellow for policies ("When X, then Y" format), blue for external systems and LLM calls, green for read models and agent observations, and red for hotspots and risks. Adding a purple for "AI-specific constraint" helps distinguish LLM behaviour rules from standard business policies.

The facilitator prompt card is a list of context-specific questions the facilitator can draw on when participant energy drops or when a particular phase is failing to surface new content. For GenAI features, prompts include: "What does the LLM see at this point that a human would not?" "What would happen if the model returned an unexpected format here?" "Is there a compliance rule that determines whether the agent can proceed without human approval?" and "What does a graceful failure look like for a user at this step?"
Post-Session Capture Artefacts

The hotspot action list is the most time-sensitive post-session artefact. It must be produced before participants leave the room. Each hotspot sticker on the board becomes a row in the list, with columns for the hotspot description, the person who raised it, the owner responsible for resolving it, the resolution approach (research spike, architectural decision, or legal review), and the deadline. Hotspots without owners and deadlines have a historical tendency to remain open indefinitely.

The bounded context map is a clean diagram produced after the session that formalises the dashed-box groupings drawn on the board. It names each context, lists its key aggregates, and identifies the context boundaries (shared kernels, anti-corruption layers, or customer-supplier relationships). For an agent system, the context map often reveals that the LLM boundary sits at an unexpected seam — for example, that the query understanding and the plan generation contexts should share a kernel to avoid translating intent representations across a boundary.
Architecture

attrs.alt: Workshop facilitation artifact pipeline across four phases

content.text: The diagram shows the four artefact phases: preparation artefacts (blue) set up the session, live artefacts (purple) structure the session in progress, capture artefacts (green) preserve the output, and backlog artefacts (red) translate the output into implementable work. Each phase feeds the next through a defined handoff.
When to Use It

A full facilitation artefact set is warranted for any workshop that involves more than four participants, has stakeholders who do not regularly collaborate, or is expected to produce backlog items that will directly drive a sprint. Lightweight artefact sets (just an agenda and a decision log) suffice for small working sessions between people who share a common context.

For GenAI feature development specifically, the AI-specific artefact extensions (model constraint canvas, prompt hypothesis sheet) should be used whenever the feature involves an LLM call, a multi-step agent workflow, or a user-facing output that is generated rather than deterministic. Omitting these extensions in favour of standard Agile artefacts leaves AI-specific risks uncaptured.
How to Implement
Define the artefact set
: Choose which artefacts are needed for the specific session type. Annotate each with owner, due time (before/during/after session), and format (digital doc, Miro frame, spreadsheet).
Build reusable templates
: Create a template library for each artefact type. Templates should be usable without modification for 80% of sessions, with clearly marked sections that can be customised for the remaining 20%.
Set up the pre-workshop package
: 48–72 hours before the session, send participants the agenda, participant brief, and scoping canvas. Set up the digital board or physical board with sticky-note colour indicators pre-printed.
Assign a dedicated note-taker
: The note-taker fills the decision log and hotspot tally sheet in real time. This must not be the facilitator — doing both roles simultaneously degrades quality of both.
Conduct the post-session debrief

: Within two hours of the session ending, the facilitator and note-taker review all hotspots, assign owners and deadlines, photograph or export the board, and distribute the session summary doc to all participants.
Convert to backlog within 24 hours

: The product manager uses the backlog conversion template to translate bounded contexts into epics, aggregates into service stories, policies into acceptance criteria, and hotspots into spike tasks. Each item is linked to the source sticky ID or board region for traceability.
Retrospect on artefacts

: After the first implementation sprint, review which artefacts were actually referenced and which were ignored. Update the template library accordingly. This feedback loop is what makes the artefact set improve over time.
Examples

The following shows a Python implementation of the core workshop artefact types — scoping canvas, hotspot tally, and backlog conversion — as structured dataclasses that can be serialised and shared across tools:

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import date
from typing import Optional, Literal
from enum import Enum
import json

class ResolutionType(Enum):
    SPIKE = "spike"
    ARCH_DECISION = "arch_decision"
    LEGAL_REVIEW = "legal_review"
    PRODUCT_DECISION = "product_decision"

@dataclass
class ScopingCanvas:
    """Pre-workshop artefact: defines scope boundaries for the discovery session."""
    feature_name: str
    target_users: list[str]
    ai_capability_description: str
    llm_model_family: str          # e.g. "claude-3.5", "gpt-4o"
    max_context_window_tokens: int
    allowed_tools: list[str]       # tools the agent may call
    required_human_checkpoints: list[str]  # policy-mandated human approval points
    explicit_exclusions: list[str]
    success_criteria: list[str]

    def to_brief(self) -> str:
        lines = [
            f"Feature: {self.feature_name}",
            f"AI model family: {self.llm_model_family} (context: {self.max_context_window_tokens:,} tokens)",
            f"Target users: {', '.join(self.target_users)}",
            "",
            "Allowed tools:",
            *[f"  - {t}" for t in self.allowed_tools],
            "",
            "Human checkpoints (mandatory):",
            *[f"  - {c}" for c in self.required_human_checkpoints],
            "",
            "Out of scope:",
            *[f"  - {e}" for e in self.explicit_exclusions],
        ]
        return "\n".join(lines)

@dataclass
class Hotspot:
    id: str
    description: str
    raised_by: str
    owner: Optional[str] = None
    resolution_type: Optional[ResolutionType] = None
    deadline: Optional[date] = None
    resolved: bool = False

@dataclass
class HotspotTallySheet:
    session_name: str
    session_date: date
    hotspots: list[Hotspot] = field(default_factory=list)

    def add(self, hotspot: Hotspot) -> None:
        self.hotspots.append(hotspot)

    def unresolved(self) -> list[Hotspot]:
        return [h for h in self.hotspots if not h.resolved]

    def unowned(self) -> list[Hotspot]:
        return [h for h in self.hotspots if h.owner is None]

    def summary(self) -> str:
        lines = [
            f"Session: {self.session_name} ({self.session_date})",
            f"Total hotspots: {len(self.hotspots)}",
            f"Unowned: {len(self.unowned())} (MUST be assigned before implementation)",
            f"Unresolved: {len(self.unresolved())}",
            "",
            "Hotspot list:",
        ]
        for h in self.hotspots:
            owner_str = h.owner or "⚠ NO OWNER"
            deadline_str = str(h.deadline) if h.deadline else "no deadline"
            lines.append(f"  [{h.id}] {h.description}")
            lines.append(f"       Owner: {owner_str} | Deadline: {deadline_str}")
        return "\n".join(lines)

# Example usage
canvas = ScopingCanvas(
    feature_name="AI Dispute Resolution Assistant",
    target_users=["Customer service agents", "End customers via self-serve portal"],
    ai_capability_description="Autonomously resolve Tier-1 payment disputes under $500",
    llm_model_family="claude-3.5-sonnet",
    max_context_window_tokens=200_000,
    allowed_tools=["transaction_lookup", "fraud_pattern_match", "refund_initiate"],
    required_human_checkpoints=["Any refund over $200", "When fraud confidence score > 0.85"],
    explicit_exclusions=["Disputes involving chargebacks", "Disputes older than 90 days"],
    success_criteria=["95% of Tier-1 disputes resolved without human", "<2s median response time"],
)
print(canvas.to_brief())

tally = HotspotTallySheet("Dispute AI Discovery", date(2025, 6, 10))
tally.add(Hotspot("H1", "Who approves refund decisions at night when ops team is offline?",
                  raised_by="Compliance Officer", owner="Head of Operations",
                  resolution_type=ResolutionType.PRODUCT_DECISION, deadline=date(2025, 6, 17)))
tally.add(Hotspot("H2", "PII masking required before sending to LLM — which fields?",
                  raised_by="Data Engineer"))  # No owner yet
print(tally.summary())
A second example shows the backlog conversion template — taking the hotspot tally and bounded context output and generating structured Jira-compatible items:

from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Literal

ItemType = Literal["epic", "story", "spike", "task", "acceptance_criterion"]

@dataclass
class BacklogItem:
    item_type: ItemType
    title: str
    description: str
    acceptance_criteria: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    source_artefact_ref: str = ""  # traceability link back to board element
    story_points: Optional[int] = None

from dataclasses import field as dc_field

@dataclass
class BoundedContext:
    name: str
    key_events: list[str]
    aggregates: list[str]
    policies: list[str]

def convert_to_backlog(
    contexts: list[BoundedContext],
    hotspots: HotspotTallySheet,
) -> list[BacklogItem]:
    items: list[BacklogItem] = []

    for ctx in contexts:
        # Each bounded context becomes an epic
        items.append(BacklogItem(
            item_type="epic",
            title=f"{ctx.name} — Agent Capability",
            description=f"All capabilities within the {ctx.name} bounded context. "
                        f"Key events: {', '.join(ctx.key_events[:3])}.",
            source_artefact_ref=f"board:context:{ctx.name}",
        ))

        # Each aggregate becomes a service/component story
        for agg in ctx.aggregates:
            items.append(BacklogItem(
                item_type="story",
                title=f"Implement {agg} aggregate",
                description=f"As the {ctx.name} service, implement the {agg} aggregate "
                            f"with command handlers and event emission.",
                source_artefact_ref=f"board:aggregate:{agg}",
            ))

        # Policies become acceptance criteria
        for policy in ctx.policies:
            items.append(BacklogItem(
                item_type="acceptance_criterion",
                title=policy,
                description=f"Policy constraint from event storming session: {policy}",
                source_artefact_ref=f"board:policy:{ctx.name}",
            ))

    # Hotspots become spikes
    for h in hotspots.hotspots:
        items.append(BacklogItem(
            item_type="spike",
            title=f"Spike: {h.description}",
            description=f"Unresolved risk identified by {h.raised_by} during discovery. "
                        f"Owner: {h.owner or 'UNASSIGNED'}.",
            source_artefact_ref=f"hotspot:{h.id}",
            story_points=3,
        ))

    return items

# Example contexts from the dispute resolution session
contexts = [
    BoundedContext(
        name="Dispute Intake",
        key_events=["Dispute submitted", "Identity verified", "Transaction data retrieved"],
        aggregates=["DisputeCase", "IdentityVerification"],
        policies=["Identity must be verified before any transaction data is accessed"],
    ),
    BoundedContext(
        name="Autonomous Resolution",
        key_events=["Fraud pattern matched", "Refund decision generated", "Customer notified"],
        aggregates=["ResolutionDecision", "RefundAuthorisation"],
        policies=["Refunds over $200 require human approval", "Log all LLM decisions for audit"],
    ),
]

backlog = convert_to_backlog(contexts, tally)
for item in backlog:
    print(f"[{item.item_type.upper():<22}] {item.title}")
Real-World Example

A healthcare SaaS company building a clinical documentation assistant ran a two-day discovery sprint for an AI feature that would generate draft clinical notes from conversation transcripts. The facilitation team prepared six artefacts before the session: a session agenda with time-boxed phases, a scoping canvas that included the target EHR systems and HIPAA constraints, a participant brief explaining Event Storming to clinicians who had never encountered the format, a Miro board with pre-loaded colour-coded sticky templates, a hotspot tally spreadsheet, and a model constraint canvas for specifying which PHI fields the LLM was permitted to see.

During the session, the model constraint canvas proved to be the highest-value artefact. It forced the clinical informatics lead and the security officer to agree, in the room, on the exact list of structured data fields that could be passed to the LLM without additional de-identification. This single constraint shaped the entire architecture of the feature — the PHI boundary became a bounded context boundary, and the de-identification service became a first-class aggregate with its own ownership team. Without the canvas, this constraint would likely have been discovered during a security review three months into implementation.
Common Mistakes
Sending the agenda but not the participant brief
— attendees who do not understand the workshop format spend the first 20–30 minutes asking the facilitator process questions instead of generating content.
Using a single artefact for both facilitation and capture

— the live board is not the capture artefact. If the Miro board is both the working surface and the final deliverable, it accumulates session debris (abandoned stickies, incorrect groupings, test entries) that obscures the real output.
Leaving hotspots without owners at session end
— the most common reason discovery hotspots remain open indefinitely is that ownership was not assigned while the relevant stakeholders were still in the room.
Delaying backlog conversion beyond 48 hours
— context degrades rapidly after a workshop. Items that were clear to participants on the day become ambiguous within 72 hours without the implicit shared understanding of the room.
Omitting AI-specific artefacts from standard Agile templates

— using a generic story template for an AI feature without fields for model constraints, prompt hypotheses, and non-deterministic behaviour risks omits the most important requirements for an LLM feature.
Best Practices

Create a team template library in a shared tool (Notion, Confluence, or a Git repository). Version-control the templates so that improvements from one session can be adopted in the next without overwriting history.

Include a "session health" retrospective slide at the end of every workshop. Spend five minutes asking: which artefacts were most useful, which were ignored, and what was missing? Feed the answers into the template library update cycle.

Mark AI-specific artefact sections visually distinct from standard Agile sections — use a different header colour or icon. This helps participants and reviewers quickly identify the LLM-specific requirements in the backlog output.

Add a traceability column to every backlog item that links it to the source board element (sticky ID, context name, or hotspot ID). This makes it possible to answer "why did we build this?" during retrospectives or when a stakeholder questions a design decision.

For distributed teams, run a 15-minute "artefact walkthrough" call the day before the session where participants preview the agenda, scoping canvas, and sticky legend. This replaces the time that would otherwise be spent on these explanations during the session itself.
Performance and Scalability

The performance of a facilitation artefact set is measured by the ratio of session time that produces backlog-ready output versus time spent on facilitation process. A well-prepared artefact set typically shifts this ratio from 40:60 (without preparation) to 75:25 (with preparation), based on practitioner observations across multiple product discovery workshops.

For organisations running multiple GenAI feature teams simultaneously, a shared facilitation artefact library enables parallel workshop execution at scale. Each team uses the same template set, producing backlog outputs in a consistent format that allows the portfolio-level product function to aggregate and prioritise across features. The alternative — each team inventing its own artefacts — produces incomparable outputs that cannot be consolidated.

Template maintenance scales as O(n) in the number of distinct session types the organisation runs. A portfolio of four session types (Event Storming, Design Sprint, threat model, retrospective) requires four template sets. The AI-specific extensions can be shared across all four as a common library of AI requirement artefacts, avoiding duplication while allowing customisation per session type.

Security and Reliability

Workshop artefacts frequently contain sensitive business requirements, compliance constraints, and descriptions of internal system architecture. The model constraint canvas, in particular, may list which PHI or PII fields are permitted to flow to an LLM — this is information that should not be publicly accessible. Artefact storage must follow the organisation's information classification policy: internal documents stored in access-controlled systems, with sharing restricted to session participants and authorised stakeholders.

For reliability, the most critical artefact is the hotspot action list. Implementing a hotspot resolution SLA — for example, every hotspot must have an owner assigned within 24 hours and be resolved within two sprints — ensures that the reliability risks discovered in the workshop are systematically addressed rather than deferred indefinitely.
Key Takeaways
Facilitation artefacts span four phases: preparation, live session, post-session capture, and backlog conversion — neglecting any phase degrades the quality of the final backlog output.
GenAI features require AI-specific artefact extensions — model constraint canvas, prompt hypothesis sheet, PHI boundary definitions — that standard Agile templates do not capture.
The hotspot tally sheet with mandatory owner assignment is the single highest-leverage artefact for preventing discovery risks from becoming production defects.

Backlog conversion must happen within 24 hours of the session — context degrades rapidly and items that were clear during the workshop become ambiguous shortly after without the shared understanding of the room.
A reusable template library version-controlled in Git enables organisations to run parallel discovery sessions across multiple AI feature teams while producing comparable, aggregatable output.

Traceability links from backlog items back to source board elements (sticky IDs, context names) make it possible to answer architectural questions months after the session by revisiting the discovery artefact rather than relying on institutional memory.
